does: This paper conducts a quantitative evaluation of two widely-deployed clinical AI systems (OpenEvidence and UpToDate Expert AI) against three frontier generalist large language models (GPT-5, Gemini 3 Pro, and Claude Sonnet 4.5) on medical reasoning tasks. Using a 1,000-item benchmark combining MedQA items testing medical knowledge with HealthBench items assessing clinician alignment, the authors measure performance across dimensions including accuracy, completeness, communication quality, context awareness, and systems-based safety reasoning. The results demonstrate that generalist models substantially outperform the specialized clinical tools, with GPT-5 achieving the highest scores, while the clinical systems show systematic deficits in multiple capability areas despite their positioning as purpose-built for clinical decision support.
connects: This work addresses a critical gap in medical AI evaluation by applying the rigorous independent benchmarking practices established in general LLM evaluation to clinical decision support systems. It builds on prior work in medical AI assessment such as MedQA and related medical knowledge benchmarks, while drawing on clinician-alignment metrics developed in broader AI safety and human-AI interaction research. The paper responds to the broader tension in AI development between specialized versus general-purpose systems, echoing earlier findings that frontier models often exhibit unexpected breadth of capability. It contributes to growing concerns about claims marketing around specialized AI systems in high-stakes domains, connecting to literature on the gap between claimed and demonstrated performance in clinical AI.
enables: These findings establish an empirical foundation for reconsidering how clinical organizations adopt and trust AI systems, suggesting that frontier generalist models warrant serious evaluation as clinical decision support tools despite not being purpose-designed for medicine. The work points toward future research on how to safely integrate frontier LLMs into clinical workflows, what additional safety requirements beyond medical knowledge are necessary, and how to conduct transparent, independent evaluation of all clinical AI systems before deployment. It opens questions about whether specialized clinical tools should be fine-tuned variants of generalist models rather than independent systems, and highlights the need for standardized, comprehensive benchmarking protocols that medical institutions should apply before adoption decisions.
